retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Care Principles -- Function -- The Primary Survey
retrieved_docs.content: An important thing to remember is that these steps can and should be repeated throughout the course of the patient’s care. For example, if the provider completed the airway and breathing portion of the exam, but the circulation portion required an intervention, restarting the primary survey from the beginning (e.g., at airway) is recommended. First, the provider’s intervention may take time to accomplish, allowing enough time to pass for the patient’s airway and breathing status to deteriorate. Second, any intervention has the potential to compromise any aspect of the primary survey. For example, if a provider determines that a central venous catheter, placed in the patient’s jugular vein, is required, they must confirm that the pleural space, vascular wall, or cardiac tissue were not violated. This could result in a tension pneumothorax, hemorrhaging, or inadequate cardiac output and deteriorate the patient’s breathing and circulatory status. Thus, the reassessment of these critical areas in sequential order is necessary. Any deterioration in the patient's clinical status mandates a repeat primary survey, including a reassessment of previous interventions to ensure they remain effective.

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Tube -- Clinical Significance -- Management of Chest Tube
retrieved_docs.content: The Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma (EAST) 2021 practice management guideline provides evidence-based recommendations for managing simple and retained hemothorax. Patel et al suggest that for hemodynamically stable patients with traumatic hemothorax, pigtail catheters can be an effective alternative to traditional large-bore thoracostomy tubes, with similar efficacy and reduced procedural pain. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Tube -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Apply evidence-based guidelines for the appropriate placement and management of chest tubes in patients with pneumothorax, hemothorax, or pleural effusion. Evaluate potential complications associated with chest tube placement and implement strategies to minimize risk and improve patient outcomes. Interpret postinsertion imaging and clinical findings to confirm correct tube positioning and assess the need for intervention or repositioning. Collaborate with the interprofessional healthcare team to ensure coordinated chest tube management, including placement, monitoring, and post-procedural care, to optimize safety and outcomes. retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pneumothorax Evaluation and Treatment -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The possibility of tension pneumothorax and concomitant hemothorax must always be considered in traumatic pneumothorax. Patients with traumatic pneumothorax often have a high association with additional thoracic and abdominal injuries. Therefore, a comprehensive trauma evaluation by emergency physicians and trauma surgeons is essential to identify and manage any concurrent injuries effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Care of a Chest Tube -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumothorax Spontaneous pneumothorax Occurs without trauma, often in individuals with underlying lung conditions or in tall, thin individuals. Chest tube drainage remains the reference first-line treatment of primary spontaneous pneumothorax. [2] Tension pneumothorax This is a medical emergency caused by progressive accumulation of air in the pleural space, leading to lung collapse, mediastinal shift, impaired venous return, and cardiopulmonary compromise. Traumatic or iatrogenic pneumothorax This results from penetrating or blunt trauma or secondary to diagnostic and therapeutic pulmonary procedures. [3] New guidelines on primary spontaneous pneumothorax suggest ambulatory approaches may be suitable. However, guidance on iatrogenic pneumothorax occurring in patients with impaired lung function, increased age, comorbidity, and frailty is lacking, and the safety profile of ambulatory management is not known. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Pneumothorax Identification Without Ancillary Testing -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: However, studies show that most pneumothorax cases do not warrant treatment, and monitoring by serial computed tomography (CT) imaging is usually sufficient. [6] [7] [8] Judicious assessment enables providers to determine if treatment is required in the prehospital setting. Inappropriate patient selection may result in unneeded further surgical management and hospitalization. When patients are selected appropriately, prehospital needle decompression is associated with lower 24-hour mortality among trauma patients. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2137
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest has a relationship that it is a subdivision of thorax, indicating that this closed‑system chest tube technique is classified within thoracic procedures. As a subdivision of thorax, closed drainage of chest is employed to evacuate air, fluid, or blood from the pleural space in conditions such as pneumothorax, hemothorax, or pleural effusion, and its categorization guides clinicians in selecting appropriate thoracic interventions and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of thorax, representing a specific thoracic intervention used to treat conditions such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. By placing a closed drainage system within the thoracic cavity, clinicians can evacuate air, fluid, or blood, thereby re‑expanding the lung and relieving the pathological processes associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure exemplifies how targeted thoracic techniques are applied to manage distinct intrathoracic disorders, highlighting the interrelationship between procedural categories (subdivision of thorax) and the diseases they address.

The three pathologies—pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse—are also associated with the term “دبيلة”. Recognizing that closed drainage of chest is employed to treat pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse underscores its importance in thoracic management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: The term “hrudník,” referring to the chest, denotes the anatomical location where pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse can develop. These three thoracic conditions are also linked with “دبيلة,” indicating an association between the clinical manifestations of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse and the entity دبيلة. Understanding that the chest (hrudník) is the site of these pathologies and recognizing their connection to دبيلة is important for accurate diagnosis and effective management of patients presenting with pleural and pulmonary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: Closed chest drainage is classified within the thoracic subdivision of procedures that involve incision of the pleura, thoracentesis, and chest drainage. This subdivision is directly linked to pleural‑cavity pathologies such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, which are considered manifestations of a broader side‑related disorder (اضطراب جنبي) and are also associated with the term دبيلة. Interventional procedures targeting the pleural space include creation of a pleuroperitoneal shunt, side‑cavity aspiration (رشف التجويف الجنبي), insertion of an underwater‑seal chest drain, and tube thoracostomy with a water seal for indications such as abscess, hemothorax, and empyema. Tube thoracostomy and chest tube insertion (تركيب أنبوب الصدر) are narrower procedures that fall under the overarching category of incision of pleura; thoracentesis; chest drainage, with the latter three actions (incision of pleura, thoracentesis, chest drainage) being directly related to chest‑tube placement. Pleural neoplasms of uncertain or unknown behavior are also linked to incision of the chest wall and pleura within this hierarchy. Understanding the network of thoracic anatomical subdivisions, pleural diseases, and their associated diagnostic and therapeutic interventions is essential for effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
retrieved_docs.content: The mediastinum is a central thoracic subdivision that serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower‑respiratory conditions classified under “other lower respiratory disease” (اضطراب منصفي), including mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية), lung‑mediastinal abscesses, and mediastinal effusion. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Chest CT—performed as a diagnostic thoracic CT protocol that may begin without contrast and continue with contrast, or as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast—provides the “has_finding_method” link to a variety of mediastinal findings: mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion. Chest CT also identifies mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, recorded with the “has_direct_procedure_site” relationship to the mediastinum, and specifies the laterality of the fistula (bilateral, right‑sided, left‑sided). Therapeutically, closed drainage of the chest is associated with the thoracic subdivision and may be employed to manage complications such as mediastinal‑pleural fistulas. Together, the anatomical context of the mediastinum, its disease categorization, the detailed CT imaging techniques, the specific radiologic findings, and the related interventional procedures constitute an integrated framework for diagnosing and treating mediastinal and thoracic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 974
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is an indirect procedure performed at the anatomical region referred to as hrudník (the chest). The hrudník serves as the location of several thoracic pathologies, including pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure is also linked to the Subdivision of thorax, a categorical segment of the thoracic cavity that itself has a relationship with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. Together, these connections underscore that closed chest drainage is specifically directed toward the chest area and the thoracic subdivision that are commonly affected by pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, highlighting the procedural relevance to these conditions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1083
retrieved_docs.content: Insertion of underwater seal chest drain is a specific technique within the broader category of procedures that involve incision of the pleura, thoracentesis, and chest drainage. Tube thoracostomy, which includes a water seal and may be used for conditions such as abscess, hemothorax, or empyema when performed as a separate procedure, is also related to incision of the pleura, thoracentesis, and chest drainage. Both procedures serve to evacuate air, fluid, or pus from the pleural space, and they share common steps of pleural incision and drainage, though the underwater seal chest drain represents a narrower, more specific application within this therapeutic domain. Understanding the relationship among these interventions aids clinicians in selecting appropriate chest drainage techniques for various pleural pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: Nasogastric tube procedures classify the nasogastric tube, indicating that the range of clinical actions, techniques, and protocols are organized around the use, insertion, management, and care of the nasogastric tube.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1686
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is linked to a specific Subdivision of thorax, indicating that this procedure is performed within a defined thoracic region. The Subdivision of thorax is encompassed by the mediastinum, the central compartment of the thoracic cavity that contains vital structures. Imaging of the mediastinum with thoracic CT scans can directly identify mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, which may be present bilaterally, on the right side, or on the left side. Recognizing these fistulas on thoracic CT scans of the mediastinum is essential for guiding the appropriate use of closed chest drainage and for planning further therapeutic interventions.
